pathophysiology_summary: Atopic dermatitis is a chronic, relapsing, itch-driven inflammatory skin disease with impaired barrier function and immune dysregulation, classically flexural (antecubital/popliteal), with xerosis, lichenification from chronic rubbing, and an itch-scratch cycle; flares are triggered by dry/cold air, irritants (soaps, fragrances) and stress. It is benign but the flare here is severe with sleep disruption. The ground-truth tasks are (1) recognising an eczema flare (flexural, xerosis, atopy history, fold-sparing) versus contact dermatitis (exposure-mapped) and scabies (finger-webs/wrists, contacts itchy, burrows), (2) crucially screening for secondary infection — bacterial (weeping/honey crusts) and eczema herpeticum (monomorphic punched-out vesicles, systemic toxicity) which need urgent systemic treatment, and (3) evidence-based management: liberal emollients (soap substitution), a topical corticosteroid of appropriate potency to active areas, trigger avoidance, and a sedating antihistamine at night only to aid sleep (antihistamines don't treat the eczema itself).

differential.evidence_basis: Flexural itchy dry patches with xerosis/lichenification and atopy history, flare with dry air.
Screen for punched-out vesicles/weeping-crusting and systemic toxicity — need urgent systemic antiviral/antibiotic.
Symmetrical flexural distribution favours endogenous atopy over exposure-mapped contact dermatitis.
Chronic flexural pattern with no finger-web/wrist involvement or itchy contacts argues against scabies.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Eczema flare leads; secondary infection (bacterial/eczema herpeticum) must be excluded before routine topical therapy.

differential.evidence_basis: No weeping/crusting/punched-out vesicles/fever, classic flexural atopic pattern — uncomplicated eczema flare.
No infection features; escalation target if they develop.
Pattern/contacts argue against; reconsider if standard therapy fails.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative infection screen, an uncomplicated eczema flare leads; emollient + TCS + trigger avoidance, with an infection safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; settles over days with TCS + emollients; needs ongoing maintenance.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is a benign eczema flare.

red_flags.description: Secondary infection — bacterial (weeping/honey crusts, pustules) or eczema herpeticum (monomorphic punched-out painful vesicles, rapid worsening, fever/malaise)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Eczema herpeticum is a dermatological emergency needing urgent systemic antiviral; bacterial superinfection needs antibiotics.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not screening for punched-out vesicles/systemic toxicity in a flaring eczematous patient.
Relying on antihistamines (which don't treat eczema) instead of emollients + topical corticosteroid; sedating antihistamine is only a night-time sleep aid.
Using too weak a steroid or too little emollient for a severe flare (or, conversely, a very potent steroid on thin skin/face for prolonged periods).

cannot_determine_via_chat: Close skin examination for subtle infection/vesicles
Skin swab/viral PCR if infection suspected
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Emollient + appropriate-potency TCS + trigger avoidance + night sedating antihistamine; urgent review/ED for eczema herpeticum/bacterial superinfection.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Liberal emollients and soap substitution are the foundation; topical corticosteroid of potency matched to site/severity to active areas; avoid irritants/triggers. Antihistamines do not treat eczema (sedating agents only for sleep). Treat/refer secondary infection promptly.
